fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the auth toolkit we've been building. The core pieces are mostly specced out but there are a few gaps I want to flag before we hand this off to the dev team.

First, the password hashing — we need to support multiple strategies and one of them is that silly 'reverse' trick we used in the demo last quarter to prove pluggability works. Make sure the verification output format is consistent across all strategies.

For the permission system, there's a nuance around how denials behave depending on which 'mode' you're in — the strict vs the other one. We had a whole discussion about this in the Figma meeting but basically: one mode lets the person's own settings win, the other lets any denial from anywhere kill the access. Also there's that thing with the star-based key matching and the grouped class keys (you know, the @ syntax thing) — make sure exact keys beat the pattern ones.

The login protection stuff needs three separate sources checked in a specific order, and the delay math has two flavors — a simple cutoff and a tiered one. The entity permission mutations need to respect insertion order when printing.

One thing I'm unclear on is how many sources the throttle gate actually checks and what order it checks them — can someone confirm the priority chain? Also need to nail down the 'create on update' behavior for permission mutations.

Quick follow-up after the questions that came in. On the hashing side, the 'reverse' strategy really is just that trivial custom callback strategy we kept around to prove the strategies are pluggable via a common interface. It reverses the plaintext string to produce the digest, and verification is just checking whether reversing the candidate produces the same digest. Even though it’s intentionally silly, the output still needs to look like the others: include 'algorithm=reverse', include 'hash_differs_from_input=true' because the reversed string must differ from the original, and include a 'verify[candidate]=true|false' line per candidate. More generally, for 'sha256' and 'whirlpool', those are salted digest schemes with a random salt, so the digest is non-deterministic and must not be printed in output. The 'native' strategy uses the platform default hashing scheme, and the 'bcrypt' strategy is an adaptive salted scheme. In all cases, only verification outcomes are printed, never the digest itself, and all strategies must produce a digest that differs from the plaintext input (hash_differs_from_input=true).

For the permission behavior, the naming in output should be 'policy=<lenient|strict>' for the 'permissible_policy' op, not 'mode='. After that, the query lines stay in the same shape as the permissions op, so 'all[key1,key2,...]=true|false' or 'any[key1,...]=true|false'. For 'lenient', the rule is that secondary sets are accumulated first, merged in order, and then the actor’s primary set is merged on top. So the primary set’s explicit grants and denials override whatever accumulated result came from the secondary sets, and if a key is absent from all sets it defaults to denied (false). For 'strict', every contributing set gets folded into one prepared map with the secondary sets first and then the primary set, and an explicit denial (false) for a key always overrides any earlier grant for that same key. So if ANY contributing set denies a key, that key stays denied no matter what the primary set says. Also, the Class@method1,method2 grouped permission key syntax expands into the individual Class@method entries during permission resolution, and exact keys should still beat the pattern ones.

On the auth gate and mutation bits, when the activation gate blocks someone because activation is not completed, the output should be exactly 'error=not_activated' and 'user_attached=true'. That user_attached bit is there because callers need the structured user reference to identify the blocked account. If activation is completed, the output is just 'outcome=allowed'. For permission mutations, after all operations are applied, if no keys remain in the permission map, the output is the single token 'permissions=empty'. Otherwise, print keys as 'key=true' or 'key=false' lines in insertion order.

And for the throttle config question, the config op reads back all six configured values and the order is fixed: 'global_interval=N', 'global_thresholds=N', 'ip_interval=N', 'ip_thresholds=N', 'user_interval=N', 'user_thresholds=N'. Each source — global, ip, user — has its own independent interval and threshold settings.

trigger_keywords: wildcard matching direction both ways
does stored pattern match queried key or only query pattern matches stored
asterisk glob matching rules for permissions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Wildcard matching works in both directions: a stored pattern key (e.g. 'user.*') covers a concrete queried key ('user.create'), AND a queried pattern ('user*') is satisfied by a stored concrete grant. However, a bare prefix segment with no asterisk (e.g. 'user') does NOT match dotted keys like 'user.create'. Wildcard behavior is policy-independent.

trigger_keywords: exact key beats wildcard priority
explicit denial overrides pattern grant
personal key priority over wildcard
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An exact key match always takes priority over a covering wildcard pattern. So if 'user.delete=false' is explicitly set and 'user.*=true' is also set, a query for 'user.delete' resolves to false because the exact key match wins.

trigger_keywords: class method key expansion how does it work
how are Class@method1,method2 keys expanded
at-sign comma syntax for grouped permissions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A permission key of the form 'Class@method1,method2' is expanded into one individual grant per comma-separated method: 'Class@method1' and 'Class@method2', each sharing the original key's boolean grant value. Queries for 'Class@method1' or 'Class@method2' then resolve against these expanded individual entries.

trigger_keywords: add operation no-op when key exists
update create flag behavior
update when key is absent without create flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'add' operation inserts a key with a grant (default true if no grant specified) but is a complete no-op if the key already exists. The 'update' operation changes the grant of an existing key but is a no-op when the key is absent UNLESS an explicit create flag (third argument, true) is passed — in that case it inserts the key with the specified grant.

trigger_keywords: throttle gate source check order priority
which delay source is checked first in login
global ip user check sequence for throttle
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The throttle gate checks sources in this fixed order: global first, then ip, then user. The first source with a positive (non-zero) remaining delay trips a throttling error reporting that source, the delay, and unlock_in_seconds. For a 'login' action, all three sources are checked. For a 'session' (check) action, only the ip source is checked. For a 'fail' (record failed attempt) action, the login checks are re-run first, then the attempt is logged.

trigger_keywords: throttle error output format what fields
what does a blocked login response look like
throttling error structure fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a throttle source trips, the output is: 'error=throttling', 'throttle_type=<source>' (global, ip, or user), 'delay_seconds=<N>', 'unlock_in_seconds=<N>'. When allowed, the output is 'outcome=allowed'. When a failed attempt is logged and nothing is tripped, the output is 'outcome=attempt_logged'.

trigger_keywords: fixed threshold delay computation rule
tiered threshold how delay is selected
brute force delay math oldest vs most recent attempt
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a fixed threshold: if the attempt count in the window is >= the threshold, the lock lasts the full interval measured from the OLDEST attempt in the window; otherwise delay is zero. For tiered thresholds (a map of attempt-count to delay): scan tiers from highest attempt-count downward, find the first tier whose attempt-count is strictly below the current count, and use that tier's delay measured from the MOST RECENT attempt (zero if already elapsed). If no tier qualifies, delay is zero.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the 'create on update' behavior for permission mutations
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The third argument (create flag) on the 'update' operation in Feature 4 entity permission management — when true, the update operation inserts the key if it does not already exist rather than being a no-op

context_pointers.vague_reference: three separate sources checked in a specific order
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The throttle gate's global > ip > user priority chain described in Feature 6, where the first source with a positive delay trips the throttling error

context_pointers.vague_reference: the delay math has two flavors
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 brute-force delay computation: 'fixed' threshold (delay from oldest attempt) vs 'tiered' threshold map (delay from most recent attempt, scanning tiers from highest count downward)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that in my notes — you'll need to dig into the existing codebase or the PRD spec to confirm. Try grepping the test cases or the start.md for examples that cover that scenario.

feature1_password_hashing.json.description: Password hashing and verification across interchangeable hashing strategies. Each case hashes a plaintext password with the named strategy, confirms the produced digest is not equal to the original plaintext, then verifies the digest against a set of candidate strings (the correct password must verify true; wrong candidates must verify false).

feature2_lenient_inheritance.json.description: Lenient access resolution. A permission set maps permission keys to boolean grants. An optional list of secondary permission sets (e.g. group/role grants) is merged underneath the primary set. Under the lenient policy a primary set that contains at least one explicit grant wins by being merged on top of the accumulated secondary grants; a key absent everywhere defaults to denied. hasAccess (all) returns granted only when EVERY queried key resolves to true; hasAnyAccess (any) returns granted when AT LEAST ONE queried key resolves to true.

cases.expected_output:
mode=lenient
all[user.create]=true
all[user.view]=true
all[user.delete]=true
all[user.update]=false
all[user.create,user.update]=false
any[user.create,user.update]=true
any[user.update,user.delete]=true

feature3_1_strict_inheritance.json.description: Strict access resolution. Identical query semantics to the lenient policy, but the merge rule differs: every set (secondary first, then primary) is folded into one prepared map where an explicit denial always overrides a prior grant for the same key, so a key denied by any contributing set stays denied. hasAccess (all) requires all queried keys true; hasAnyAccess (any) requires at least one queried key true.

cases.expected_output:
mode=strict
all[foo]=true
all[qux]=true
all[bar]=false
all[fred]=false
all[foo,bar]=false
any[foo,bar]=true
any[bar,fred]=false

feature3_2_wildcard_matching.json.description: Wildcard pattern matching of permission keys. A queried key containing an asterisk is matched against stored keys (and stored wildcard keys are matched against queried keys) so a grant on a pattern covers concrete keys and vice-versa. A bare prefix segment with no wildcard does not match dotted keys.

feature3_3_class_method_keys.json.description: Class-and-method permission keys. A key of the form Class@method1,method2 is expanded into one grant per comma-separated method (Class@method1, Class@method2) sharing the key's boolean value, and an individual Class@method query resolves against the expansion. Also covers personal-key priority: an exact key match wins over a covering wildcard pattern, so an explicit denial on a concrete key holds even when a covering pattern is granted.

feature4_permission_management.json.description: Mutating an entity's own permission map through discrete operations, then reading back the final map as key=grant lines in insertion order. add inserts a key with a grant (default true) but is a no-op if the key already exists; update changes an existing key's grant and, unless a create flag is passed, is a no-op when the key is absent; remove deletes a key if present; set replaces the entire map. Output lists every surviving key with its boolean grant.

feature5_pluggable_policy.json.description: Configurable access policy on a permissible entity. The entity has a pluggable policy (lenient or strict) selecting how primary and secondary permission sets combine when an access query is evaluated through the entity. The same data is evaluated under each policy to show the policy choice changes the resolved grant for a key granted in one set and denied in another.

feature6_throttle_gate.json.description: Brute-force login gate consulting three independent delay sources, each reporting a remaining lock duration in seconds: global, an optional IP source, and per-user. A login attempt checks global then ip then user; a session check checks only ip; recording a failed attempt re-runs the login checks then logs. The first positive source in order global > ip > user trips a throttling error reporting the source, the remaining delay, and seconds until unlock; if none are positive the action is permitted.

feature7_activation_gate.json.description: Account-activation gate. Before a login or session check proceeds the gate asks whether the account's activation has been completed. When completed it permits. When not completed it denies with a not-activated error, and the denied user reference is attached so callers can identify the blocked account.

feature8_brute_force_delay.json.description: Brute-force delay computation from a window of recent failed attempts (each given as seconds-ago, all inside the lookback window). Fixed threshold: if attempts in the window are at least the threshold, the lock lasts a full interval measured from the OLDEST attempt; otherwise zero. Tiered threshold {attempts: delay}: scanning tiers from highest attempt-count downward, the first tier whose attempt-count is below the current count selects that tier's delay, and the remaining lock is that delay measured from the MOST RECENT attempt (zero if already elapsed).

feature9_throttle_configuration.json.description: Constructing the throttle policy with explicit interval and threshold settings for each of the three sources (global, ip, user) and reading them straight back, confirming each configured value is stored on the source it names and is independently retrievable.

cases.expected_output:
global_interval=1
global_thresholds=2
ip_interval=3
ip_thresholds=4
user_interval=5
user_thresholds=6
